patient.name: Maya Okafor
patient.chiefComplaint: Acute respiratory distress, unable to pass suction catheter through trach

history.hpi: 6-year-old female with chronic bronchopulmonary dysplasia (former 25-week preemie), ventilator-dependent at baseline, with a chronic tracheostomy (4.0 mm cuffed, in place for 4 years). Parents report the child has been congested for 2 days and had thick secretions this morning. When mom attempted routine suctioning, the catheter would not pass. Child is now in acute distress with cyanosis.
pastMedical: Severe BPD on chronic home ventilator via tracheostomy
history.lastOralIntake: Tube feeds via G-tube running continuously at low rate
history.events: Two days of thickened respiratory secretions. Routine suctioning this morning failed. Child is in acute distress.

scene.dispatch: Medic 12, respond code 3 to 1430 Ridgecrest Lane for a 6-year-old female with tracheostomy, respiratory distress, can't pass a suction catheter.
scene.location: Suburban home, main floor bedroom set up with medical equipment and home ventilator. Child in her medical bed.
scene.safety: Scene safe. Home has suction, pulse oximeter, spare trachs, go-bag.
scene.bystanders: Both parents present, highly experienced with the equipment. Mother is nurse. Has a 'go-bag' ready with spare trachs and suction catheters.
visualCues: Chronically ill-appearing young girl in a specialized bed
No air movement heard through the trach
Tracheostomy tube visible at the anterior neck
Mother holding a spare 4.0 trach and stating 'she needs it changed'
Go-bag with suction catheters, lubricant, saline, and spare trachs at the bedside

phases.name: Arrival — Severe Respiratory Distress via Tracheostomy
phases.description: Ventilator-dependent pediatric tracheostomy patient with complete obstruction. Per protocol, in a peds trach patient in significant distress, the problem is PLUGGING or DISLODGEMENT until proven otherwise. Systematic DOPE troubleshooting: disconnect vent, pass suction, if catheter won't pass, emergently change the trach.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Tracheostomy in place but occluded; no air movement through it
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Severe retractions without air entry; bradypneic and failing
otherFindings: Existing trach is a 4.0 cuffed (size imprinted on the flange)
Mother has a spare 4.0 trach and a go-bag
Child has a native upper airway (no laryngectomy) — could be orally intubated as backup
Ventilator alarm confirms no exhaled tidal volume

expectedActions.action: Disconnect the home ventilator — eliminate equipment as a cause
Remove inner cannula if present — can plug independently of outer
Provide passive oxygenation — high-flow O2 over nose/mouth AND over the stoma to avoid desaturation during troubleshooting
Instill 1-3 mL sterile saline into the trach and attempt to pass an 8 French suction catheter (size = 2x trach size = 8 Fr for a 4.0 trach)
If suction catheter will NOT pass, recognize complete obstruction and proceed directly to emergent tracheostomy change
expectedActions.rationale: In significant distress with inability to pass suction, don't delay — change the tube.

expectedActions.action: Prepare a NEW 4.0 cuffed trach (same size) from the family's go-bag; lubricate the obturator; have a 3.5 trach and a 3.5 mm ETT ready as backup
Apply cardiac monitor, SpO2, waveform capnography (will confirm successful ventilation after replacement)

phases.name: Emergent Tracheostomy Change
phases.description: Crew commits to emergent tracheostomy replacement. Deflate cuff, remove ties, pull old trach, insert lubricated new trach with obturator, remove obturator, begin BVM ventilation, confirm placement.

transitions.condition: Replacement difficult or tube not advancing

clinicalPresentation.airway: Obstructed trach being removed; mature stoma visible
otherFindings: Obstructed tube coming out with clear thick plug visible

expectedActions.action: Deflate cuff with 10 mL syringe, cut or release ties, gently remove the obstructed trach
Insert lubricated new 4.0 cuffed trach with obturator following the curve into the stoma; remove obturator immediately after insertion
Begin BVM ventilation via the new trach with pediatric-size bag and 100% oxygen
Confirm placement with waveform capnography, bilateral breath sounds, chest rise, and improving SpO2
Inflate the cuff minimally for BVM seal
Secure the new trach with trach ties

phases.name: Default — Successful Replacement
phases.description: New trach in place. BVM confirms good ventilation with bilateral breath sounds and return of EtCO2 waveform. Child pinks up rapidly. Transport to pediatric ED.

clinicalPresentation.airway: New 4.0 cuffed trach, secured with ties, patent
clinicalPresentation.breathing: BVM-assisted with symmetric chest rise; SpO2 climbing
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Withdrawing from pain; becoming more responsive

expectedActions.action: Continue BVM ventilation titrated to spontaneous efforts and capnography
Bring the family's go-bag including additional spare trachs, suction supplies, and vent settings documentation
Invite a parent to accompany — they are the expert on this child's equipment and normal baseline
Transport to pediatric ED with early notification

phases.name: Branch: Difficult Replacement
phases.description: The new trach will not advance due to stoma narrowing. Crew tries smaller trach, reposition with neck extension, 90-degree insertion technique. If still failing, oral intubation from above (this patient has a native airway) is the rescue.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Stoma narrowing or false passage; tube not advancing
otherFindings: Stoma present but narrowed; risk of false passage

expectedActions.action: Extend the neck with a shoulder roll and re-attempt
Attempt insertion at 90 degrees from the neck axis (lateral) then rotate back along the long axis
Try a smaller size: 3.5 trach or 3.5 mm cuffed ETT through the stoma (bougie-assisted if available)
If stomal replacement fails, orally intubate from above (patient has native upper airway — this is appropriate here)
Cover the stoma with an occlusive dressing during BVM from above to minimize leak

criticalActions: Recognize pediatric trach patient in distress = plug or dislodgement until proven otherwise
Provide passive oxygenation over nose/mouth AND stoma
Attempt saline and suction catheter pass (size = 2x trach size)
If catheter will not pass, change the tracheostomy emergently with same-size replacement
Confirm new trach placement with waveform capnography
Bring the family go-bag and invite a parent to accompany
If replacement fails, try smaller size OR orally intubate from above if native airway present
Transport to pediatric ED with notification
expectedActions: Document existing trach size from flange imprint
bonusActions: Use bougie to assist ETT placement through stoma
Recognize laryngectomy patients cannot be intubated orally (this patient can)

learningObjectives: Apply the MATC Tracheostomy Management protocol to a plugged trach
Execute emergent trach change under time pressure
Use the DOPE mnemonic to troubleshoot trach patients
Leverage family expertise and the home go-bag
Recognize when oral intubation is a viable rescue
discussionQuestions: What does DOPE stand for and how does it structure your troubleshooting?
How do you determine the correct suction catheter size?
When is it appropriate to orally intubate a trach patient?
How do you handle a 'difficult trach' that won't advance through a narrowing stoma?
What role does the family's go-bag play in this emergency?
commonPitfalls: Forgetting to disconnect the home ventilator
Not providing passive oxygenation to the upper airway during stoma work
Using too-large a suction catheter and failing to pass
Delaying trach change while cycling through suction attempts
Forgetting to deflate the cuff before removing the old trach
keyTakeaways: In a peds trach in distress, think plug or dislodgement FIRST
DOPE is a rhythm — walk through it systematically
The family knows the child — leverage their expertise and equipment
Same-size first, then smaller, then oral intubation
